It preserves the DYNEVDCODE key set state, extracts active and in-edit Evidence Type versions, version snapshots, and related generated/processing/custom rulesets, but not localizable resource bundles. Notes that published custom ruleset changes are only picked up after republishing. Before upload, codetables must be regenerated. Also covers a required property for generated tab sections and gives command-line execution setup and example parameters.

children.title: Extractor input parameters
children.summary: Defines extractor input parameters for dynamic evidence configuration extraction. Covers how to run the extractor from command line, Ant script, or Eclipse, and notes the parameter format uses -D name=value with no spaces. Mandatory parameters include component, serverDir (for Eclipse), lowerKey, and upperKey. Also introduces evidence type extraction strategy parameters, with extractAll=true extracting all active dynamic evidence types and overriding other strategy settings.

children.title: Extracted artefacts
children.summary: This section explains what artefacts the extractor generates for dynamic evidence configuration. It covers input parameters that control extraction, then lists output types: CTX codetable files and DMX entity files with blob/clob subfolders. It details extracted entities and dependencies, including EvidenceTypeDef, version, security, AppResource, and Creole ruleset tables, plus what each contains (localizable descriptions, tab/menu/navigation XML, rulesets, links). Useful if you need the extraction output structure or entity dependency rules.

Describes the Dynamic Evidence Configuration Extractor, which exports runtime Dynamic Evidence configuration into rebuildable Cúram artefacts (DMX/CTX/XML blobs, tab/section files). Covers output layout, key replacement to avoid primary-key clashes, optional links to Application/Product/Integrated Cases, and three selection modes: all types, named list, or code prefix. Explains command-line/Eclipse execution and required parameters. Lists extracted codetables/entities, active and in-edit versions, rulesets, and notes that localizable bundles aren’t extracted and codetables must be regenerated before upload.

children.title: Features of the Dynamic Evidence Metadata Loader
Running the downloader
children.summary: Explains how to run the Dynamic Evidence Metadata Downloader from either the command line or Eclipse. It gives example invocation syntax for a batch file or Ant script, and for Eclipse using `curam.util.impl.BatchLauncher` with the batch operation `DynamicEvidenceMetadataDownloader.downloadMetadata`. It also notes the required input parameters (`location`, `evidenceType`, `effectiveDate`) and that command-line arguments must be passed with `-D`.

children.title: Downloader input parameters
children.summary: This section explains how to run the Dynamic Evidence Metadata Downloader and what input parameters it requires. It covers command-line and Eclipse execution, showing example invocations for batch/Ant scripts and BatchLauncher. It also states that all parameters must be passed with the `-D` option when running from the command line. The section begins the list of mandatory parameters, but their details are not included in the excerpt.

children.title: Running the uploader
children.summary: Explains how to run the Dynamic Evidence metadata uploader from either the command line or Eclipse. It gives example commands using `uploadDynamicEvidenceMetadata` or its Ant script, and Eclipse run configuration details for `curam.util.impl.BatchLauncher` with two arguments. It also lists uploader input parameters, emphasizing that `location` is mandatory and must point to a valid XML file; invalid paths or contents cause errors.

children.title: Uploader input parameters
children.summary: Describes uploader input parameters and how to run the Dynamic Evidence Metadata Uploader. Key mandatory parameter is `location`, the XML file location in the file system; missing folder/file, invalid contents, or insufficient privileges cause errors. Also references `evidenceType` (logical Dynamic Evidence Type name) and `effectiveDate` (version effective date) as input parameters used when launching from command line or Eclipse, including required `-D` syntax and batch launcher/ant examples.

Explains the Dynamic Evidence Metadata Loader, a tool for importing and exporting dynamic evidence metadata. It has two features: Download, which writes metadata for a specified effective date from the runtime database to XML in the file system; and Upload, which reads XML metadata from the file system and updates the runtime database. Describes how to run both tools from the command line or Eclipse using BatchLauncher/Ant scripts, and lists mandatory input parameters such as location, evidenceType, and effectiveDate, including error conditions for invalid paths or files.

children.title: Generated artefacts
EvidenceType codetable entries
children.summary: This section explains auto-generated EvidenceType code table entries for new Dynamic Evidence Types. It covers how the codetable code is formed (environment variable prefix plus unique number, default prefix DET), that the description comes from the Dynamic Evidence Type name, and that entries use the server locale and are removed if the type is cancelled. It also warns about locale issues in multi-locale deployments and notes the related application property resource naming for evidence type descriptions.

children.title: Property resources for evidence types
children.summary: Dynamic evidence types automatically create a localized property application resource for the evidence type description. The file is generated when the Evidence Type is created, and its name follows the pattern `DynEvd_EvidenceType_<logicalName>.properties`. This section sits alongside other generated artefacts such as codetable entries and explains that dynamic evidence administration creates resources that affect runtime behavior and localization.

children.title: Security identifiers and security groups
children.summary: Explains how dynamic evidence types create and manage security identifiers and security groups. Each type is linked to an administrator-chosen security group, and three SIDs are auto-generated for create, modify, and read operations using the group name. These SIDs are added to both that group and EVIDENCEGROUP if present, and are removed when the type is cancelled. The section also notes related generated CER rule sets, propagator configurations, and property/UIM resources created on activation and removed on cancellation.

children.title: Cúram Express Rule (CER) Rule Sets
children.summary: Activation of each dynamic evidence type version generates CER rule sets, which are removed if the version is cancelled. This section also notes related generated artifacts: security identifiers and groups for evidence maintenance, propagator configurations tied to the rule sets, property application resources for localized UI strings, and dynamic UIM resources created on first caseworker access. It points to other sections for more detail on dynamic evidence rule sets and localization.

children.title: Propagator configuration
children.summary: This section explains automatically generated artifacts for dynamic evidence, including security groups and SIDs for create/modify/read permissions, CER rule sets, propagator configurations, property application resources, and dynamic UIM resources. It notes when each is generated or removed (on activation, first page access, or cancellation), naming conventions such as the DynEvd_ prefix, and that administrators should not edit or remove generated resources.

children.title: Properties resources for evidence type versions
children.summary: Describes resources generated for dynamic evidence type versions. On activation, each version creates a default-locale property application resource containing all localizable UI strings, named DynEvd_ + logical evidence type name + effective date; administrators must not modify it, and it is removed on cancellation. It also notes dynamic UIM pages and their property resources, generated on first caseworker access, all prefixed DynEvd_, also protected from editing/removal and removed when the version is cancelled.

children.title: Dynamic UIM resources
children.summary: Dynamic evidence type versions generate dynamic UIM pages and matching property application resources on first access in the case worker application. All files use the “DynEvd_” prefix. Administrators should not edit or remove these resources, and they are deleted when the dynamic evidence type version is cancelled.

children.title: Tab configuration and the Application Resource Store
children.summary: Explains how dynamic evidence tab, nav, and optional menu configuration resources are generated in the Application Resource Store for each Dynamic Evidence Type Version, including naming based on type code plus effective date and cleanup on cancellation. Also covers dynamic generation of shared domain definitions in DynEvdDomains.xml, regenerated on startup and when versions are activated/cancelled. Notes the EvidenceTypeDefinition entity stores behavior data, and ends with a checklist-style summary of generated artefacts (codetable entries, properties resources, SIDs, CER rulesets, propagator configs).

children.title: Domain definitions
children.summary: This section explains dynamically generated domain definitions for Dynamic Evidence. They are stored in the Application Resource Store in a shared file, DynEvdDomains.xml, generated on startup and regenerated whenever a Dynamic Evidence Type Version is activated or cancelled; admins should not edit it. It also notes the EvidenceTypeDefinition entity, which stores behavior info for each evidence type, and introduces a checklist-style summary of generated artefacts for dynamic evidence.

children.title: The Evidence Type Definition entity
children.summary: This section explains the EvidenceTypeDefinition entity, which stores behavior information for each dynamic evidence type and gets one database entry per new type. It also summarizes generated artefacts for dynamic evidence, including tab/nav/menu configuration resources in the Application Resource Store, the shared DynEvdDomains.xml domain resource, and other generated items such as codetable entries, properties resources, security identifiers, CER rulesets, and propagator configurations. It is useful for understanding what system artifacts are created, updated, or removed when evidence types are activated or cancelled.

children.title: Summary of generated artefacts
children.summary: This section lists generated artefacts for Dynamic Evidence as a checklist. It covers tab, nav, and optional menu configuration resources in the Application Resource Store, updates to Application section files on activation/cancellation, shared domain definitions in DynEvdDomains.xml, and the EvidenceTypeDefinition database entity. It also summarizes artefact types and multiplicities: EvidenceType codetable entries, per-type properties resources, three security identifiers, CER rulesets, and propagator configurations.

This section explains the automatic artefacts created for Dynamic Evidence administration and when they are generated, updated, or removed. It covers EvidenceType codetable entries, evidence type property resources, security groups and SIDs, CER rule sets, propagator configurations, dynamic UIM resources, tab/nav/menu resources in the Application Resource Store, shared domain definitions in DynEvdDomains.xml, and the EvidenceTypeDefinition database entity. It also warns administrators not to edit generated resources and notes locale considerations and cancellation cleanup.

children.title: Dynamic Evidence application properties
children.summary: This section lists Dynamic Evidence-related artefacts and their multiplicity in AppResource store and the EvidenceTypeDefinition table, then defines application properties for Dynamic Evidence. Key properties include the auto-generated evidence type code prefix (max three characters, default DET) and the LRU cache maximum size for in-progress user operations across evidence versions (default 1000). It also notes that Cúram application properties are managed in the system administration application.

children.title: Compliance for dynamic evidence
Java API
children.summary: Dynamic Evidence exposes public Java APIs for custom rule sets and application code; do not implement or subclass its interfaces/classes unless Javadoc explicitly allows it. It also ships four infrastructure rule sets—DynamicEvidenceRuleSet, EvidenceCalculatedAttributesRuleSet, EvidenceSummaryRuleSet, and EvidenceValidationRuleSet—which must not be modified because they form the contract with custom processing rule sets. The section also notes the default “DET” codetable prefix for evidence types and how to change it via application property, plus brief steps for sample widget source code setup.

children.title: Infrastructure rule sets
children.summary: Dynamic Evidence includes four infrastructure rule sets—DynamicEvidenceRuleSet, EvidenceCalculatedAttributesRuleSet, EvidenceSummaryRuleSet, and EvidenceValidationRuleSet—that are part of the contract with custom processing rule sets and should not be modified. The section also notes that Dynamic Evidence public Java APIs may be used by custom code, but implementations or subclasses should not be provided unless Javadoc explicitly allows it. It mentions the default “DET” evidence type prefix and the property to change it, plus brief sample widget setup steps.

children.title: Dynamic evidence types prefix
children.summary: Dynamic Evidence type codetable entries are auto-generated with a default “DET” prefix. Customers should not use this prefix for custom Dynamic Evidence Types; administrators should set a different prefix via the application property `curam.dynamicevidence.type.code.prefix`. This section also notes that Dynamic Evidence public Java APIs are documented in Javadocs and must not be implemented or subclassed unless explicitly allowed, and that four infrastructure rule sets ship with Dynamic Evidence and should not be modified.

This section covers Dynamic Evidence compliance and upgrade-related settings. It documents the application property `curam.dynamicevidence.type.code.prefix`, whose default is `DET`, to control auto-generated Dynamic Evidence Type codetable prefixes (max three characters), and notes the in-progress cache size property `curam.dynamicevidence.inprog.cache.max.size` with default 1000. It is useful for administrators and developers needing to adjust evidence type code generation or understand Dynamic Evidence behavior during upgrades and user transfers.

children.title: Source code for the sample widgets
children.summary: Gives brief setup steps for a sample dynamic evidence widget: define a domain and plugins in DomainsConfig.xml, implement the plugin classes under the component’s javasource folder, then rebuild the client. Notes that the example domain name can then be used in dynamic evidence configuration and refers to the Custom Widget Development Guide. The rest of the pages are legal notices, copyright/license terms, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Explains how administrators configure dynamic evidence in Cúram using the Dynamic Evidence Editor and Dynamic Evidence List page. Covers dynamic evidence types vs. versions, effective dates, version lifecycle, activation and validation, version copying, case type association, and runtime behavior when entering evidence. Also summarizes generated artefacts, security setup, application properties, infrastructure rule sets, localization, metadata import/export tools, and the sample widget setup. Useful for understanding admin workflows and the configuration objects created for dynamic evidence.

Covers how to configure evidence for use by caseworkers: enabling deleted-evidence display in Evidence Type/Object Type lists, creating case-type-specific evidence dashboard groups with optional required evidence, and enabling end dating during evidence creation for dynamic and non-dynamic evidence. Also introduces dynamic evidence administration: types vs. versions, effective dating and lifecycle behavior, case-type association, and generated artefacts, security, properties, rule sets, localization, import/export, and sample widget setup.
